agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A production logistics system with established invariants around Shipment-Carrier assignment. The product team requests an 'auto-split' feature that would automatically divide overweight shipments across multiple carriers without supervisor approval.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods to be transported, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times from assignment through dispatch."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The entity responsible for physically transporting a Shipment."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The binding of exactly one Carrier to exactly one Shipment. Once established, this binding is locked and can only be changed by a Supervisor."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role with authority to override locked Carrier assignments — the only role permitted to reassign."},{"term":"Sub-Shipment","definition":"(Proposed) A child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment that exceeds the weight threshold. Does not exist in the current domain model."},{"term":"Auto-Split","definition":"(Proposed) An automatic system action that decomposes a single Shipment into multiple Sub-Shipments, each assigned to a different Carrier, triggered when weight exceeds 500 kg."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment leaves the facility. The system guarantees every dispatched Shipment has exactly one Carrier."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data consistency and state integrity constraint. If violated, the system cannot determine who is responsible for a Shipment, creating accountability gaps, liability exposure, and operational chaos.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in the system, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization integrity constraint. If violated, arbitrary actors can break the chain of custody, creating security and accountability failures. The lock exists to prevent unauthorized mutation of a committed assignment.","testable_assertion":"Any Carrier reassignment operation that is not initiated by a Supervisor must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is the dispatch-gate invariant. A Shipment without a single accountable Carrier cannot leave the facility. Violating this means goods leave with unclear responsibility — a corrupt state.","testable_assertion":"The dispatch operation must fail if the Shipment does not have exactly one assigned Carrier."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A 500 kg shipment is split into two 250 kg sub-shipments, but one sub-shipment's carrier becomes unavailable before dispatch.","expected_behavior":"Unclear under the proposed feature. The existing model would require a Supervisor to reassign. The auto-split proposal is silent on this."},{"scenario":"A shipment weighs 1500 kg and must be split into 4 sub-shipments, but only 2 carriers are available.","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The proposal assumes carriers are always available for auto-assignment."},{"scenario":"A parent shipment is split, then the customer requests cancellation of the original shipment.","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Must all sub-shipments be cancelled? What is the relationship between parent and children?"},{"scenario":"A sub-shipment itself exceeds 500 kg after splitting (e.g., a single indivisible item weighing 600 kg).","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Recursive split is impossible if goods are indivisible."},{"scenario":"Auto-split assigns a carrier, then a supervisor reassigns the parent shipment's carrier — what happens to sub-shipments?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The parent-child lifecycle is not specified."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Potential efficiency gains from automated splitting could reduce manual overhead and speed up heavy-shipment processing. However, if implemented incorrectly, split shipments with unclear carrier accountability create liability exposure — if goods are damaged or lost, which carrier is responsible?","operational":"Introduces a new Shipment lifecycle (parent/child) that does not exist today. Every downstream system — tracking, billing, proof of delivery, SLA monitoring, insurance — must understand sub-shipments. This is not a feature; it is a domain model evolution.","user":"Customers currently track one shipment. Splitting creates multiple tracking entities. Without careful UX design, customers will see their single order fragmented into pieces they did not request, generating confusion and support tickets.","risk":"HIGH. The feature as specified directly contradicts two production invariants. Shipping it as described would either (a) break the invariants silently, creating corrupt state, or (b) require redefining the core domain model, which has cascading effects across the entire system.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must now manage parent-child shipment relationships, partial dispatches, and multi-carrier coordination for what was previously a single unit of work."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their exclusive authority over reassignment is bypassed by the auto-split mechanism. This undermines the control model they rely on."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Now receive sub-shipments that are fragments of a larger whole. Carrier SLAs, liability, and proof-of-delivery processes must account for partial shipments."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"A single order may now arrive in multiple deliveries from different carriers, with different tracking numbers and potentially different delivery times."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Billing model must handle split shipments — is it one invoice or multiple? How are carrier fees allocated across sub-shipments?"},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Chain of custody documentation, insurance coverage, and liability assignment all change when a single shipment becomes multiple."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The specification contains a direct contradiction with two established production invariants (one-carrier-per-shipment and supervisor-only reassignment). The feature request does not acknowledge these conflicts or propose how to resolve them. Additionally, the request is silent on the parent-child shipment lifecycle, billing implications, customer communication, and carrier availability constraints. The spec quality is low — it describes a desired outcome without addressing the domain model changes required to support it.","high_confidence_areas":["The feature as literally specified violates BR-1, BR-2, and BR-3","The domain model must evolve to support this — it cannot be bolted on"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends sub-shipments to be first-class Shipments or a new entity type","Whether 'without supervisor involvement' means supervisors should have no visibility or just no blocking approval","How parent-child shipment lifecycle should work","Billing, tracking, and SLA implications"],"assumptions":["The existing invariants (BR-1, BR-2, BR-3) are intentional and represent real business constraints, not accidental technical choices","The 500 kg threshold is a business policy, not a physical constraint"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly contradicts invariant BR-1 (one Carrier per Shipment). The feature requires multiple carriers to handle what is currently a single Shipment. Either the Shipment concept must be redefined to support a parent-child hierarchy where each child independently satisfies BR-1, or the invariant must be relaxed — which would affect every Shipment in the system, not just heavy ones.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Carriers","Customers","Finance","Legal"],"options":["A: Introduce a Sub-Shipment entity that is a first-class Shipment, each satisfying BR-1 independently. The parent becomes a logical grouping, not a dispatchable unit.","B: Relax BR-1 to allow many-to-many Carrier-Shipment relationships system-wide.","C: Reject the feature as incompatible with the current domain model and propose an alternative (e.g., manual split by Supervisor)."],"recommendation":"Option A. It preserves all three invariants by reframing the problem: splitting creates new Shipments, each with exactly one Carrier. The parent Shipment becomes a non-dispatchable aggregate that tracks the group. This is a domain model evolution, not an invariant violation."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature bypasses Supervisor authority (BR-2) by automatically assigning carriers to sub-shipments without supervisor involvement. This directly contradicts the established control model where only Supervisors can change carrier assignments.","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Operations","Compliance"],"options":["A: Auto-split is treated as initial assignment (not reassignment), so BR-2 does not apply — Supervisors still control subsequent changes.","B: Auto-split requires Supervisor approval (contradicts the feature request but preserves BR-2).","C: Create a new 'System' actor with assignment authority scoped exclusively to auto-split events."],"recommendation":"Option A, combined with an audit trail. The initial carrier assignment for a newly created sub-shipment is not a reassignment — it is a first assignment. BR-2 (lock after assignment) still applies afterward. This preserves the invariant while enabling automation."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The feature request does not define the lifecycle relationship between a parent Shipment and its sub-shipments. What happens to the parent? Is it cancelled, archived, or does it remain active? Can sub-shipments be independently modified, cancelled, or further split?","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Customers","Engineering"],"options":["Parent transitions to a 'Split' terminal state and sub-shipments take over the lifecycle independently.","Parent remains active as a tracking aggregate with sub-shipments as children."],"recommendation":"Needs product team clarification before any implementation begins."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"The 500 kg threshold is stated without context. Is this a physical constraint (vehicle capacity), a regulatory limit, an insurance boundary, or an arbitrary business policy? The answer determines whether it is an invariant or a configurable policy, and whether it should be hardcoded or admin-configurable.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Compliance","Engineering"],"options":["Hardcode 500 kg as an invariant if it reflects a physical or regulatory constraint.","Make it configurable if it is a business policy that may change."],"recommendation":"Clarify with product team and, if regulatory, with legal/compliance."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The auto-split feature as specified requires a single Shipment to have multiple Carriers simultaneously.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"violation":"The auto-split feature assigns carriers automatically without Supervisor involvement, bypassing the locked-assignment control model.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Once a Carrier is assigned, only a Supervisor can reassign."},{"violation":"If the parent Shipment is dispatched in its split state with multiple carriers, the dispatch-gate invariant is violated.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3: At dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier."}]}
